text: Psychologist Daniel Simons made inattentional blindness famous with his 1999 gorilla experiment: observers asked to count basketball passes frequently failed to notice a person in a gorilla suit walking through the scene. The finding is not about vision — participants saw perfectly well. It is about consciousness. The visual system processed the gorilla; the attentional system decided it was irrelevant and excluded it from awareness. What reaches consciousness is not what is present but what has been selected as task-relevant. Simons's work demonstrated that our sense of perceiving the full scene is an illusion — awareness is always narrower than we believe.
question: In Simons's gorilla experiment, why did participants fail to notice the gorilla?

options.text: Their visual acuity was impaired by the counting task
The gorilla appeared for too short a time to register
The attentional system excluded it from awareness as task-irrelevant
The gorilla was positioned at the edge of the visual field
